2: Since the energy crisis of the 1970's, the interest of European countries in the production of biomass for energy purposes has increased considerably (Walle et al., 2007a; Guidi et al., 2009; Mizey and Racz, 2010), together with the concern about conse- quences of fossil fuels combustion on the climate change and oil prices. In fact, Northern and Central European countries began to promote energy crops ever since. However, in Southern countries, such as Italy, the promotion of bioenergy is more recent and still moderate (Fiala et al., 2010).
Italy's interest to increase the contribution from agro-energy to national energy demand is increasing in recent years and, nowa- days new incentive-providing instruments have been approved
such as the so-called Renewable Energy Certificates (Gasol et al., 2010), specifically focussed on electricity and thermal energy generation.

Bioenergy plantations are expected to become the most important source of biomass for energy purposes on a global scale (Berndes et al., 2003; Mizey and Racz, 2010). More specifically, the use of forest wood was identified as a potential source of biomass for energy in several studies (up to about 115 EJ year in 2050) (Berndes et al., 2003; Hämäläinen et al., 2011). Although in past years the interest of biomass was focused on heat and electricity production, the new available technologies and the increasing cost of oil have currently driven the interest to liquid biofuels produc- tion (and feedstock composition) (Guidi et al., 2009).

European farmers are attracted to energy crops, following the most recent changes in the Common Agricultural Policy and the rapid development of the bioenergy sector (Spinelli et al., 2008). Amongst cropping modules, short rotation coppices seems to best reflect the expectations of farmers, who are used to short return

times and do not show interest for traditional wood plantations which require up to 30 year intervals (Spinelli et al., 2008; Börjesson and Tufvesson, 2011). Short rotation coppices are wood crops usually defined as silvicultural practices, in which fast- growing tree species are grown under intensive management (weed control, fertilizers application and irrigation) in order to obtain high biomass yields (Bergante et al., 2010; Johansson and Karació, 2011). However, their study is at a preliminary study level in Italy (Manzone et al., 2009) although over the last 20 years, supported by favourable grant programmes, short rotation coppices have been established on about 6500 ha, mainly in the Po Valley area (Fiala et al., 2010; Gasol et al., 2010; Bergante et al., 2010; Fiala and Bacenetti, 2011). The first Italian region to receive subsidies for the establishment and management of these crops was Lombardy (Spinelli et al., 2008). Thereafter, other regions have followed but none is yet offering the same level of subsidies. Nowadays, 4000 ha are dedicated to short rotation coppices culti- vation in Lombardy (Fiala et al., 2010). Moreover, this region has gained relevance due to the establishment of numerous biomass thermoelectric power plants (Bergante et al., 2010).

The most extensively species used in short rotation coppices are poplar and willow, although there is some research focussed on birch, maple and alder as potential alternatives (Walle et al., 2007a, b; Guidi et al., 2008, 2009; Johansson and Karació, 2011; Uri et al., 2002). In Italy, the most common species are poplar, willow, black locust and eucalyptus, but most plantations consist of poplar clones (Fiala et al., 2010; Bergante et al., 2010). In fact, plantations in Northern Italy are established almost exclusively with poplar (there is around 100,000 ha of poplar not only dedicated to energy production but also to wood for furniture and paper) since sites are fresh enough, farmers are familiar with the species and specific clones are available for biomass production (Spinelli et al., 2008).

Poplar culture in Italy is intensive and rotations are usually shorter than in other countries. The rotation period of poplar coppice plantations is dependent on the initial plant density and growth rate (Kauter et al., 2003). This crop has proved to be extremely well suited for biomass production due to its rapid juvenile growth, high photosynthetic capacity, superior growth performance and large wood biomass production in a single growing season (Pontailler et al., 1999). Poplar cultivation presents other benefits such as the prevention of soil erosion and protection of soil water (Pandey and Srivastava, 2010). Moreover, it can serve as a temporal habitat to many species (Cardias Williams and Thomas, 2006; Walle et al., 2007b; Archaux et al., 2010). Finally, poplar is also interesting due to its high quality timber, which can be used as sawn timber, veneer, panels and pulpwood production (Cardias Williams and Thomas, 2006).

Many choices made by farmers are likely to impact biodiversity, such as the choice of poplar clone(s), the stem density at plantation, the type and length controls (chemical or mechanical) and the rotation length. The choice of all these variables would convert the poplar cultivation towards sustainable biomass production system (Archaux et al., 2010; Bergante et al., 2010). In recent years, the development of new poplar clones as well as the improvement of in cultivation techniques have made possible to obtain considerable biomass yield increases (Bergante et al., 2010).

Poplar plantations can be carried out on different management systems with different cutting times. Therefore, the management regime can be crucial for attaining high yields: short cutting frequency (1-2 years) and medium cutting frequency (>5 years). They are known as very short rotation coppice (VSRC) and short rotation coppice (SRC), respectively. They are both coppices; that is, stumps cut back to produce shoots. However, remarkable differ- ences are identified on both biomass production regimes, which will
be presented in this paper later concerning the planting systems: plants density, distance between stumps and cuttings planted.

Although nowadays the larger part of Italian short rotation crops are based on 2-years cuts (VSRC), the best quality of biomass comes from 5-years plantations (SRC) and it is possible that in the near future SRC plantations will be more widespread due to the lower ash content in the bark (Guidi et al., 2008). Therefore, it is necessary to deepen the knowledge concerning the ability of adaptation of poplar clones to different environmental conditions and cultural techniques, especially for bioenergy dedicated plantations since they affect yields and chemical content (Guidi et al., 2009): the longer the rotation, the better the result in both biomass yields and cellulose content under the same conditions.

The aim of this work was to assess the environmental effects of poplar cultivation in the Po Valley (Italy), where the Italian agri- cultural industry is concentrated (Spinelli et al., 2008), under two different management regimes in commercial plantations (VSRC and SRC) during the 2010 season. Poplar was chosen to be analysed since it is the most extensive short rotation coppice in Italy (Spinelli et al., 2008). In addition, once the best management system has been identified, the potential future production of poplar will be also analysed from an environmental point of view considering 2020 and 2030 as case studies, in order to compare them with the current scenario (2010).

Moreover, a sensitivity assessment was carried out considering only manure fertilization instead of a combination of manure and mineral fertilization since application of manure can help to diminish the use of fossil energy and related CO2 emissions required for manufacturing industrial fertilizers but under VSRC management (Di Candilo et al., 2010).

The general approach of this study is not only to gather the inventory data for these short rotation scenarios for poplar and quantify their environmental effect as well as to identify the 'hot spots' and to discuss some possible improvement opportunities due to the relevance of this crop in the Italian energy profile, but also to identify the best production management system in order to obtain biomass for energy purposes with the best quality and yield.

10: Since the energy crisis of the 1970's, the interest of European countries in the production of biomass for energy purposes has increased considerably (Walle et al., 2007a; Guidi et al., 2009; Mizey and Racz, 2010), together with the concern about conse- quences of fossil fuels combustion on the climate change and oil prices. In fact, Northern and Central European countries began to promote energy crops ever since. However, in Southern countries, such as Italy, the promotion of bioenergy is more recent and still moderate (Fiala et al., 2010).
Italy's interest to increase the contribution from agro-energy to national energy demand is increasing in recent years and, nowa- days new incentive-providing instruments have been approved
such as the so-called Renewable Energy Certificates (Gasol et al., 2010), specifically focussed on electricity and thermal energy generation.

Bioenergy plantations are expected to become the most important source of biomass for energy purposes on a global scale (Berndes et al., 2003; Mizey and Racz, 2010). More specifically, the use of forest wood was identified as a potential source of biomass for energy in several studies (up to about 115 EJ year in 2050) (Berndes et al., 2003; Hämäläinen et al., 2011). Although in past years the interest of biomass was focused on heat and electricity production, the new available technologies and the increasing cost of oil have currently driven the interest to liquid biofuels produc- tion (and feedstock composition) (Guidi et al., 2009).

European farmers are attracted to energy crops, following the most recent changes in the Common Agricultural Policy and the rapid development of the bioenergy sector (Spinelli et al., 2008). Amongst cropping modules, short rotation coppices seems to best reflect the expectations of farmers, who are used to short return

times and do not show interest for traditional wood plantations which require up to 30 year intervals (Spinelli et al., 2008; Börjesson and Tufvesson, 2011). Short rotation coppices are wood crops usually defined as silvicultural practices, in which fast- growing tree species are grown under intensive management (weed control, fertilizers application and irrigation) in order to obtain high biomass yields (Bergante et al., 2010; Johansson and Karació, 2011). However, their study is at a preliminary study level in Italy (Manzone et al., 2009) although over the last 20 years, supported by favourable grant programmes, short rotation coppices have been established on about 6500 ha, mainly in the Po Valley area (Fiala et al., 2010; Gasol et al., 2010; Bergante et al., 2010; Fiala and Bacenetti, 2011). The first Italian region to receive subsidies for the establishment and management of these crops was Lombardy (Spinelli et al., 2008). Thereafter, other regions have followed but none is yet offering the same level of subsidies. Nowadays, 4000 ha are dedicated to short rotation coppices culti- vation in Lombardy (Fiala et al., 2010). Moreover, this region has gained relevance due to the establishment of numerous biomass thermoelectric power plants (Bergante et al., 2010).

The most extensively species used in short rotation coppices are poplar and willow, although there is some research focussed on birch, maple and alder as potential alternatives (Walle et al., 2007a, b; Guidi et al., 2008, 2009; Johansson and Karació, 2011; Uri et al., 2002). In Italy, the most common species are poplar, willow, black locust and eucalyptus, but most plantations consist of poplar clones (Fiala et al., 2010; Bergante et al., 2010). In fact, plantations in Northern Italy are established almost exclusively with poplar (there is around 100,000 ha of poplar not only dedicated to energy production but also to wood for furniture and paper) since sites are fresh enough, farmers are familiar with the species and specific clones are available for biomass production (Spinelli et al., 2008).

Poplar culture in Italy is intensive and rotations are usually shorter than in other countries. The rotation period of poplar coppice plantations is dependent on the initial plant density and growth rate (Kauter et al., 2003). This crop has proved to be extremely well suited for biomass production due to its rapid juvenile growth, high photosynthetic capacity, superior growth performance and large wood biomass production in a single growing season (Pontailler et al., 1999). Poplar cultivation presents other benefits such as the prevention of soil erosion and protection of soil water (Pandey and Srivastava, 2010). Moreover, it can serve as a temporal habitat to many species (Cardias Williams and Thomas, 2006; Walle et al., 2007b; Archaux et al., 2010). Finally, poplar is also interesting due to its high quality timber, which can be used as sawn timber, veneer, panels and pulpwood production (Cardias Williams and Thomas, 2006).

Many choices made by farmers are likely to impact biodiversity, such as the choice of poplar clone(s), the stem density at plantation, the type and length controls (chemical or mechanical) and the rotation length. The choice of all these variables would convert the poplar cultivation towards sustainable biomass production system (Archaux et al., 2010; Bergante et al., 2010). In recent years, the development of new poplar clones as well as the improvement of in cultivation techniques have made possible to obtain considerable biomass yield increases (Bergante et al., 2010).

Poplar plantations can be carried out on different management systems with different cutting times. Therefore, the management regime can be crucial for attaining high yields: short cutting frequency (1-2 years) and medium cutting frequency (>5 years). They are known as very short rotation coppice (VSRC) and short rotation coppice (SRC), respectively. They are both coppices; that is, stumps cut back to produce shoots. However, remarkable differ- ences are identified on both biomass production regimes, which will
be presented in this paper later concerning the planting systems: plants density, distance between stumps and cuttings planted.

Although nowadays the larger part of Italian short rotation crops are based on 2-years cuts (VSRC), the best quality of biomass comes from 5-years plantations (SRC) and it is possible that in the near future SRC plantations will be more widespread due to the lower ash content in the bark (Guidi et al., 2008). Therefore, it is necessary to deepen the knowledge concerning the ability of adaptation of poplar clones to different environmental conditions and cultural techniques, especially for bioenergy dedicated plantations since they affect yields and chemical content (Guidi et al., 2009): the longer the rotation, the better the result in both biomass yields and cellulose content under the same conditions.

The aim of this work was to assess the environmental effects of poplar cultivation in the Po Valley (Italy), where the Italian agri- cultural industry is concentrated (Spinelli et al., 2008), under two different management regimes in commercial plantations (VSRC and SRC) during the 2010 season. Poplar was chosen to be analysed since it is the most extensive short rotation coppice in Italy (Spinelli et al., 2008). In addition, once the best management system has been identified, the potential future production of poplar will be also analysed from an environmental point of view considering 2020 and 2030 as case studies, in order to compare them with the current scenario (2010).

Moreover, a sensitivity assessment was carried out considering only manure fertilization instead of a combination of manure and mineral fertilization since application of manure can help to diminish the use of fossil energy and related CO2 emissions required for manufacturing industrial fertilizers but under VSRC management (Di Candilo et al., 2010).

The general approach of this study is not only to gather the inventory data for these short rotation scenarios for poplar and quantify their environmental effect as well as to identify the 'hot spots' and to discuss some possible improvement opportunities due to the relevance of this crop in the Italian energy profile, but also to identify the best production management system in order to obtain biomass for energy purposes with the best quality and yield.
11: Life Cycle Assessment approach (LCA) is defined as a method- ology for the comprehensive assessment of the impact that a product has on the environment throughout its life cycle (from extraction of raw materials through manufacturing, logistics and use to scrapping and recycling, if any), which is known as a "from cradle to grave" analysis (ISO 14040, 2006). LCA is an objective process to evaluate the environmental burdens associated with a product by identifying natural resources consumption and emissions to environmental compartments, and to identify and implement opportunities to attain environmental improvements.
12: In order to study the impacts of Italian poplar plantations, two productive scenarios were constructed, based on records from commercial plantations: VSRC and SRC. The purpose of this LCA analysis was to determine the best current production manage- ment from an environmental point of view in order to obtain biomass for energy purposes with the best quality and yield since, although poplar is an extensive crop in Italy over the last 20 years, especially for electricity and district/domestic heating generation, there is controversy concerning the best production regime (Guidi et al., 2008, 2009). For both current assessed scenarios, the func- tional unit (FU) of the study was defined as an average hectare of poplar plantation under the management practices defined for the
specific scenario. The FU selection is in agreement with other short rotation crop LCA studies (Heller et al., 2004; Gasol et al., 2009; González-García et al., 2011).

The environmental study was completed with two sensitivity analysis: i) the application of only manure fertilization instead of manure and mineral fertilizing under VSRC in accordance with Di Candilo et al. (2010) in order to estimate the environmental improvements assuming an increment in the biomass yield and, ii) the estimation of environmental effects of poplar production in the future considering 2020 and 2030 seasons. In both future scenarios, the current management practices of poplar biomass production (irrigation, agrochemical applications and intensive activities) under the best environmental impact results were assumed but with potential improvements in the clones (higher survival rates and better chemical composition). Concerning the biomass yield, it was assumed 1.5 times and 2 times the current yield (2010), respectively based on reliable data in Italy (Joyce, 2010). For these sensitivity studies, the FU was an oven dried ton (o.d.t) of poplar biomass. The selection of this FU is in agreement with other short rotation crop LCA studies (González-García et al., 2011).

14: <header>2.2. Description of the commercial plantations under assessment </header>

As mentioned before, planting systems are different depending on the cutting frequency. Both current commercial plantations under study are located in Ostiano (Lombardy) in the Po Valley area (45° long. N, 10° 15' lat. E). This region has a sub-continental climate, with rainfall mainly concentrated in the autumn and spring (mean 745 mm yr-1) and the medium annual temperature is 12 °C. The texture of the soil is: 52% sand, 30% silt and 17% clay. The clone considered in this study, AF2, is specific for bioenergy purposes and it was obtained by crossing Populus deltoides and Populus nigra.

The analysed energy poplar systems dedicated to biomass production for energy purposes can be divided in several phases: field preparation, management and harvesting and, field recovery. All these phases included different processes which have been identified in detail in both scenarios. Fig. 1 shows the scheme of VSRC (10-years rotation with 2-years harvesting intervals) and SRC (10-years rotation with 5-years harvesting intervals) systems with the corresponding processes involved.
Table 1 shows the agricultural labours covered throughout the life cycle of both poplar plantations and corresponding years of activity.

Before planting (1st year) in both crops, soil was previously fertilized with manure with 50 t ha-1 ploughed and harrowed. Manure (from cows) applied presents 80-82% moisture and an average composition of 0.45 kg N 100 kg manure, 0.20 kg P kg manure and 0.35 kg K 100 kg- manure. This manure is applied in February when average maximum temperature is 7.3 and average minimum temperature is 1.0 °C. No rainfall occurred in the five days after the manure application and 20 mm of water occurred in the 6th day. All this climatic information is important since incorporation of organic fertilizer into the soil or rainfall after application leads to a reduction of the maximum potential NH3 loss. Moreover, air temperature is a key parameter for the NH3 volatili- zation (Brentrup et al., 2000).

Planting is performed in different planting systems between both management regimes. Unrooted 22 cm cuttings of AF2 were planted in the VSRC plantation at a density of 5560 cuttings ha-¹ with a distance of 50-cm between rows and 280-300 cm within rows. Concerning SRC, 2 m plant rods of AF2 were planted at a density of 1150 cuttings ha-Superscript(1) and the distance between stumps in the row is quite similar to the width of lanes (a distance of 280-300 cm between rows and 280-300 cm within rows). In both plantations the lanes allow the use of conventional tractors.

After planting (also in the 1st year), chemical weed control is carried out by spraying glyphosate (31%) at a total rate of 4 kg ha-1 once and twice in VSRC and SRC respectively. Pest management required one treatment in both case studies of gluphosinate- ammonium (11.3%) at a total rate of 2 kg ha-1 Mechanical weed control is also carried out in the 1st year after planting and consists of harrowing the soil (twice in VSRC and once in SRC). Finally, and due to poplar growth requires high water consumption, irrigation was performed in the plantations. A total of 400 m³ ha of water were applied. Due to the existence of rivers nearby, water is pumped from the river to the plantation by gravity so no pumps connected to tractors are required.

VSRC presents 10-year rotation with harvesting carried out every 2-years. Therefore, harvesting steps take place on the 2nd, 4th, 6th, 8th and 10th years by means of a combine harvester. This machine simultaneously cuts and chips the biomass. After that, the fresh poplar chips are collected and loaded by trailers coupled to a farm tractor and are finally stored. Nitrogen fertilization is applied after each harvesting step at a rate of 80 kg N of urea (46%) so in total, 320 kg N ha-1 Once again, herbicide (4 kg ha-Superscript(1) yr of gluphosinate-ammonium) and pesticide (2 kg of delta- methrin) application is carried out once after each harvesting step together with mechanical weed control and irrigation (400 ha yr-1). Following the last harvest (10th year), the field is recovered by means of a hoeing machine. Concerning the SRC plantation, the total length is 10 years. However, cutting occurs every 5-years. Harvesting operations include the use of a set of machines: a harvester for the felling, a tractor coupled with a trailer equipped with a pincers to transport the biomass out of the field and a chipper. Chemical weed control is limited to the following years: once in year 2, twice in year 6 and once in year 7, with an application rate of 2 kg ha- Nitrogen fertilization is applied in the year after the 1st harvesting step (6th year) at a rate of 200 kg N ha of urea (46%). Mechanical weed control is also performed twice on the 2nd year, once the 3rd, twice the 6th and 7th years and finally, once on the 8th year of cultivation. Pest control is established the 2nd, 6th and 7th years once per year (2 kg deltamehrin ha in total). Irrigation is performed the 2nd, 5th and 6th years (400 m³ ha yr-1). Following the last harvesting (10th year), the field is recovered.

The biomass yield for short rotation crops (not only poplar but e.g. black locust) tends to be higher under SRC management instead of VSRC according to the literature (Fang et al., 1999; Guidi et al., 2009; González-García et al., 2011). In this study, a total biomass production of 200 o.d.t ha (~55% moisture content) and 173 o.d.t ha-¹ 55% moisture content) for SRC and VSRC have been assumed, respectively.

Carbon stored by the biomass was estimated considering carbon content in the dried biomass of 50% (Fiala et al., 2010) and both above ground and below ground biomass (roots and stools) were taken into account in this calculation since the latter represents a potential pool for carbon storage. Finally, it is important to note that we have assumed zero change in the overall soil carbon content under the poplar cultivation regimes due to its specific dependency on factors such as manage- ment practices, climate, previous cropping regime and the limita- tion of available data for poplar plantations and Italian conditions.

29: Allocation is one of the most critical issues in LCA. It is required for multi-output processes and the selection of an allocation approach for processes that produce more than one co-product can have a strong effect on the results. In this cradle-to-gate analysis allocation was not required since we have only considered the

production of useful above ground biomass harvested to be deliv- ered to Italian energy producers. Therefore, all environmental effects were assigned to that amount of energy biomass. Finally, forest waste generated in the harvesting step (such as leaves, stools and branches) was not computed in the analysis since they commonly remain in the plantation.
30: <header>3. Environmental results for current scenarios: VSRC and SRC </header>

An LCA for both energy poplar scenarios was carried out according to the CML 2 baseline 2000 V2.05 method to quantify the environmental impact (Guinée et al., 2001). This method results in the definition of an environmental profile for the assessed production by quantifying the environmental effects on different categories. The following impact categories have been assessed: abiotic depletion (ADP), acidification (AP), eutrophication (EP), global warming (GWP), ozone layer depletion (ODP) and photo- chemical oxidants formation (POP). In addition, toxicological impact categories (human toxicity (HT), fresh water aquatic

ecotoxicity (FE), marine aquatic ecotoxicity (ME) and terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE)) were also analysed although the LCA community has not yet reached a full consensus on the characterization models for their definition. In addition, an energy analysis was performed using the cumulative energy demand method (CED) (VDI- Richtlinien, 1997) as a further impact category. The CED repre- sents the entire energy demand, valued as primary energy, which arises in connection with production, use and disposal of an economic good. LCA software SimaPro 7.10 developed by PRé Consultants (2011) was used to construct the LCA model and undertake the impact assessment calculation.

The results for the characterization step for the two current scenarios are shown in Table 2. According to these results, the production of poplar biomass presents a better environmental profile under SRC conditions instead of VSRC in almost all impact categories (except in ADP, ODP, HT and POP), with improvements from 4% to 22% depending on the category. This result fits in with other related studies were short rotation crops such as poplar and black locust were assessed (Bergante et al., 2010; González-García et al., 2011). The results from this comparative study are impor- tant for the future of dedicated energy crops not only in Italy but also in other European countries since although nowadays 2-years cut plantations (VSRC) are more widespread, the best quality energy biomass comes from 5-years cut plantations (SRC). Addi- tionally, SRC presents higher economic benefit (Bacenetti and Fiala, 2011; Fiala and Bacenetti, 2011; Guidi et al., 2009).
Figs. 2 and 3 show the contributions from agricultural processes to the environmental impact categories under assessment for VSRC and SRC systems, which will be analysed next.

37: The production of renewable biomass (above and below ground) presents a carbon sink due to CO2 taken up by the biomass during its growth. This absorption of CO2 into the biomass 'offsets' GHG emissions from the poplar energy dedicated plantation activities such as fuel combustion in tractors, agrochemicals production and field emissions such as N20 from nutrients application, which contribute to the net GWP score and are included in the analysis. In these poplar scenarios, 6135 kg fossil CO2 and 5812 kg fossil CO2 are derived from involved activities in SRC and VSRC respectively. However, 440 t CO2 and 380.6 t CO2 are fixed by the biomass growth in SRC and VSRC systems respectively (they do not have the same biomass yield, being up to 13.5% higher under SRC regime).

Concerning the hot spots in terms of GHG emissions, mechanical weed control, urea production, harvesting and biomass collection are the most important contributors due to the diesel consumption in all these activities. With respect to the contributing substances, N20 and fossil CO2 dominate the GHG emissions with ratios of 17.4% and 79.2% respectively in VSRC and 16.6% and 80.3% respectively in SRC.

44: In this category, the consumption of energy from different non- renewable sources (fossil and nuclear) and renewable sources are analysed in detail in terms of GJ (lower heating value) equivalent. The energy requirement in the SRC scenario is slightly higher than in the VSRC, around 0.3% (Table 2), mainly due to higher diesel consump- tion in activities such as harvesting, biomass extraction (by means of a pincer connected to a tractor) and chipping into wood chips. All these activities requires 578.4 kg diesel. On the contrary, biomass harvesting and extraction under VSRC regime is carried out using exclusively a combine harvester which requires 440 kg diesel in total. However, it is important to remark that the dose of nitrogen based fertilizer required under VSRC is higher than under SRC conditions. The results are most favourable for the SRC scenario at 43 vs 37 for the VSRC. It is important to remark at this point that biomass production was higher in the SRC scenario (200 o.d.t ha-1 than in the VSRC scenario (173 o.d.t so the amount of energy saved per ha was higher in the first one: 3700 GJ VS 3200 GJ, based on a lower heating value of 18.5 GJ o.d.t-¹ of harvested poplar biomass (Fiala et al., 2010).

47: The replacement of fossil fuels with biomass for energy production is an important strategy promoted by the EU to mitigate fossil CO2 emissions, as well as to conserve the future availability of fossil fuels (von Blottnitz and Curran, 2007; Mizey and Racz, 2010). If Europe wants to implement its ambitions with bioenergy for large-scale power and liquid biofuels generation, it is essential that the production of the feedstock (that is, the main raw material) is carried out under sustainable conditions. In this context, wood perennial crops, such as willow, poplar, eucalyptus, black locust and grasses such as miscanthus, are obtaining special attention from an environmental and economic point of view (Manzone et al., 2009).

In Italy, for the last five years numerous hectares of energy plantations have been established, mainly in the Po Valley, the region under assessment in this paper. The main species planted on this region is poplar, followed in a smaller proportion by willow and back locust (Bergante et al., 2010). Hence, this paper focussed on commercial plantations of poplar dedicated to biomass production for energy purposes.

The production of biomass as well as its quality (i.e. cellulose, lignin, ash contents) depends considerably on several factors which must be taken into account when a poplar plantation is going to be established: genetic characteristics (the clone used), soil conditions

(pH, organic matter content, ...), local climate conditions (temper- ature, rainfall, cultural techniques (irrigation, manuring, and management regimes (cutting cycles). Therefore, the latter have been taken into account assuming the same clone planted on two commercial poplar plantations located in the Po Valley.

According to the environmental results, poplar plantations carried out over 10 years under SRC management that is, 5-years harvesting intervals, present a better profile than if harvesting is performed in 2-years intervals in almost all the impact categories under assessment. Commonly, the longer the rotation, the better the result is in biomass yield (Fang et al., 1999, 2007; Guidi et al., 2009). In this study, the biomass yield in both commercial planta- tions (20.0 ha- yr and 17.3 o.d.t ha yr for SRC and VSRC respectively) was considerably high in comparison to other yields reported. However, these reported yields correspond to experi- mental plots (there are no results available for commercial plan- tations), where the average biomass production is from 3.5 o.d.t ha-Superscript(1) yr-Superscript(1) in Belgium (Walle et al., 2007a) to 18.4 o.d.t ha-¹ in Italy (Guidi et al., 2009). It is obvious that all the factors mentioned before are responsible for these results.

Taking into account the environmental results achieved in this paper, special attention should be paid on several agricultural processes in order to improve the environmental profile of both management regimes under assessment. In accordance to other related studies, and as observed in Figs. 2 and 3, nutrient application and agricultural activities such as mechanical weed control, har- vesting and biomass collection can be considered the hot spots in all impact categories due to energy consumption and derived emis- sions. Gasol et al. (2007) assessed the Ethiopian mustard as potential crop for energy purposes in southern Europe and identified fertil- izers production and related diffuse emissions from their applica- tion the hot spot in categories such as AP and EP. Moreover, toxicity categories were influenced by combustion emissions from diesel in agricultural activities. González-García et al. (2011) assessed the production of black locust under different management regimes in order to obtain energy biomass. Once again fertilizers production was found to be the main hot spot in terms of SO2 and NO emissions (acidifying substances). Moreover, agricultural machinery use (especially harvesting step and harrowing) was also important in terms of NOx emissions. Concerning PO, production machinery was the most remarkable contributor. Finally, Gasol et al. (2009) ana- lysed the production of poplar in Spain under experimental condi- tions. According to this study, field operations related to the first year management and establishment are the most critical from an energy point of view, especially fertilization due to the production of fertilizers (energy intensive process).

Concerning the energy balance, 85.8 GJ ha and 85.6 GJ ha-¹ are required all over the ten years plantation under SRC and VSRC regimes. Gasol et al. (2009) reported 85.2 GJ ha in a 15-years plantation with 5-years cut intervals and 13.5 o.d.t It is important to remark that this energy input includes all energy consumed in all agricultural activities as well as all input produc- tion processes. Energy output stored in biomass was 3200-3700 GJ ha in our poplar plantations and 3931 GJ ha-Superscript(1) in Gasol et al. (2009). However, and according to Gasol et al. (2009), there are other more efficient energy crops from an energy point of view such as Ethiopian mustard.

52: <header>4.1. Sensitivity assessment: mineral fertilizing vs manuring </header>

Numerous studies have identified nitrogen mineral fertilization as an intensive process and one of the most energy demanding processes all over the cycle of energy dedicated biomass plantations (Di Candilo et al., 2010; Gasol et al., 2009). The promotion of the use of manure instead of industrial fertilizers could help to reduce the

environmental profile of poplar cultivation not only in terms of energy inputs but also in terms of diffuse emissions from fertilizing. Di Candilo et al. (2010) assessed the productivity, energy and carbon cycle implications derived from substitution of mineral fertilizers in poplar plantations (2-years cut intervals) located in the Po Valley by cattle manure as nutrient input and under two different manure rates and compositions. These results were compared with poplar cultivation under no fertilizing conditions. According to this study, it was possible to increase the biomass yield up to 22% if industrial fertilizer is applied (120 kg N ha urea and 120 kg P2O5 ha superphophate) and up to 30% if cattle manure (200 m³ ha-1) is considered in comparison with no fertilizing conditions. If manure application is compared to industrial fertilizing, the biomass yield is increased 6% under manuring conditions.

Therefore, a hypothetical alternative scenario to our current VSRC regime (2-years cut intervals too) has been proposed based on manure application substituting the use of urea dose (320 kg N ha-1 and biomass yield of 184.3 o.d.t ha The current VSRC system was considered instead of the SRC system in order to minimize errors since, although the environmental profile is better for the SRC (Table 2), the biomass yield tends to be different under different cutting intervals (Guidi et al., 2009) and Di Candilo et al. (2010) based their analysis under VSRC regime.

Thus, in this hypothetical scenario the application in the first plantation year of 50 t ha of cattle manure was assumed (as in the current VSRC system) and instead of 80 kg N ha of urea in the 3rd, 5th, 7th and 9th years, a total of 71.1 t ha of cattle manure was considered, also in four applications (density 400 kg m-3. composition: 4.5 kg N The production of this cattle manure was excluded from the system boundaries since it was considered as a waste from farms.

According to the comparative environmental results and assuming 1 o.d.t as the functional unit in the comparison (Fig. 4), remarkable improvements can be achieved if nitrogen based industrial fertilizer is replaced by cattle manure. The highest reductions were achieved for AP (61.6%) and EP (57.8%), due to the absence of contributions from industrial fertilizer production and diffuse emission from its application. As mentioned in the previous section, both impact categories were considerably affected in the current system by the diffuse NH3 emissions derived from urea application into the soil. The emission factor required for this calculation was taken from Brentrup et al. (2000) and in accordance to this study, 15% of total applied mineral N in form of urea is volatilized in Italy. The rate of NH3 volatilized from cattle manure is considerably lower (Brentrup et al., 2000), which is reflected in AP and EP. Another impact category that undergoes considerable reductions is TE, which was affected in the current system by the production of urea. In this hypothetical study, a reduction of up to 45.5% is achieved. Concerning CED, and in accordance with Di Candilo et al. (2010), the shift from industrial to organic fertil- lizing helps diminish the use of energy ( 19.8%), thanks to the absence of fertilizers production.

Therefore, a hypothetical alternative scenario to our current VSRC regime (2-years cut intervals too) has been proposed based on manure application substituting the use of urea dose (320 kg N ha-1 and biomass yield of 184.3 o.d.t ha The current VSRC system was considered instead of the SRC system in order to minimize errors since, although the environmental profile is better for the SRC (Table 2), the biomass yield tends to be different under different cutting intervals (Guidi et al., 2009) and Di Candilo et al. (2010) based their analysis under VSRC regime.

Thus, in this hypothetical scenario the application in the first plantation year of 50 t ha of cattle manure was assumed (as in the current VSRC system) and instead of 80 kg N ha of urea in the 3rd, 5th, 7th and 9th years, a total of 71.1 t ha of cattle manure was considered, also in four applications (density 400 kg m-3. composition: 4.5 kg N The production of this cattle manure was excluded from the system boundaries since it was considered as a waste from farms.

According to the comparative environmental results and assuming 1 o.d.t as the functional unit in the comparison (Fig. 4), remarkable improvements can be achieved if nitrogen based industrial fertilizer is replaced by cattle manure. The highest reductions were achieved for AP (61.6%) and EP (57.8%), due to the absence of contributions from industrial fertilizer production and diffuse emission from its application. As mentioned in the previous section, both impact categories were considerably affected in the current system by the diffuse NH3 emissions derived from urea application into the soil. The emission factor required for this calculation was taken from Brentrup et al. (2000) and in accordance to this study, 15% of total applied mineral N in form of urea is volatilized in Italy. The rate of NH3 volatilized from cattle manure is considerably lower (Brentrup et al., 2000), which is reflected in AP and EP. Another impact category that undergoes considerable reductions is TE, which was affected in the current system by the production of urea. In this hypothetical study, a reduction of up to 45.5% is achieved. Concerning CED, and in accordance with Di Candilo et al. (2010), the shift from industrial to organic fertil- lizing helps diminish the use of energy ( 19.8%), thanks to the absence of fertilizers production.

56: <header>4.2. Sensitivity assessment: future production of poplar biomass </header>

The growing interest on biomass for energy purposes is driving the studies towards the development of new clones more resistant to diseases and adverse climatic conditions in order to increase the biomass yield and improve the biomass composition (higher cellulose and hemicellulose as well as lower lignin and ash contents) (Guidi et al., 2009) in order to obtain higher energy yields.
Fig. 4. Comparative environmental profile of the current VSRC system and a hypo- thetical VSRC system under only manure fertilization. The current VSRC system serving as the baseline (Index = 100).

In this sensitivity analysis, two future scenarios were proposed (2020 and 2030 seasons) in addition to the current one (2010), assuming commercial plantations carried out under SRC regimes since they presented the best environmental results (Table 2) and biomass yields according to numerous studies (Guidi et al., 2008, 2009; González-García et al., 2011).

For the future scenarios, it has been assumed that new high yielding clones of poplar with a favourable composition for conversion to energy fuels and higher biomass yield will be avail- able. However, no differences have been taken into account con- cerning agricultural field activities. Concerning the biomass yield, the following values were assumed: 200 o.d.t ha in 2010, 300 o.d.t in 2020 (1.5 times the 2010 yield) and 400 o.d.t ha in 2030 (2 times the 2010 yield), which were taken from Joyce (2010) as a result of the European EnergyPOPLAR project (2011).

The normalization phase in an LCA study allows to compare all environmental impacts using the same scale as well as to add the normalization values of impact categories in order to obtain a unique value per scenario. Fig. 5 shows the normalization values of the ten impact categories under assessment (ADP, AP, EP, GWP, ODP, HT, FE, ME, TE and POP). The situation in Western Europe has been taken into account as the reference for all impact categories (data from year 1995) as this is the most complete list available (Guinée et al., 2001). Regarding the energy requirements (CED), the CML methodology does not include it, therefore this flow indicator has been only considered at the characterization step.
According to the comparative results shown in Fig. 5, the envi- ronmental profiles associated to the agricultural production process improve up to 33.3% and 50.0% in 2020 and 2030 due to the
Fig. 5. Sensitivity assessment of the normalization index (per o.d.t) for SRC2010, SRC and SRC2030-

increment of the biomass yield. Therefore, if the production of energy, such as heat or ethanol from this biomass sources, is ana- lysed, considerable reductions on the environmental profiles all over the LCA could be achieved since the composition of the biomass should improve.

In addition, the most significant impact categories identified in the normalization step were: ADP, AP, EP, GWP, FE and ME. These categories are usually affected by fertilizers application, diesel use and combustion in agricultural machinery. Therefore, future work should focus on reducing fertilizer doses and carrying out agricul- tural labours under a more efficient way, reducing diesel consumption.

60: Two commercial and representative poplar plantations run under two different management regimes along 10 years in the Po Valley (Italy) and dedicated to biomass production for energy purposes were assessed in detail from an environmental point of view in order to identify the one that allows obtaining biomass under a more sustainable way. The management regimes are based on 2-years cutting intervals (VSRC) and 5-years cutting intervals (SRC). Several differences were identified not only in biomass yield but also in terms of fertilizers requirement and intensive agricul- tural activities. According to the environmental results, SRC pres- ents the best profile in seven of the eleven environmental impacts under assessment and the key processes in both regimes were identified: nutrient application and activities such as mechanical weed control, harvesting and biomass collection due to the energy consumption and derived emissions.

Improvement alternatives were proposed based on these results and two sensitivity analyses were carried out based on the use of only cattle manure as nutrient input under a VSRC regime and on the increment of biomass yield in the future (2020 and 2030 seasons) due to better clones but under an SRC regime. The sensi- tivity analyses showed remarkable environmental improvements in all the categories under assessment due to the absence of contributions from the application of industrial fertilizers and production, and highest biomass yield. Therefore, in a short period of time poplar plantations dedicated to energy purposes should be driven towards SRC regimes instead of VSRC.
